text:
I want to build an LLM-powered auto-triage bot for our support tickets — mode: **stress**, depth: **standard**. Let's start with the thesis.

Here's my restatement of the idea, as the strongest honest version of it: *You want to build a bot that uses an LLM to automatically triage incoming support tickets — reading each one and categorizing, prioritizing, and/or routing it to the right team or queue — so that tickets move faster and human agents spend less time on manual sorting.*

Is that an accurate restatement, or would you correct something in it?
Yeah, that's right — the goal is to cut down the manual triage work so tickets land with the right team faster and agents aren't burning time just sorting queues.

Good — thesis confirmed. Now I'll press on it, one question at a time.

When you say the bot "triages" tickets — does it actually move/assign the ticket on its own, or does it suggest a team/priority that a human then confirms? Where, if anywhere, does a human step in before a ticket lands somewhere?
It suggests — nothing moves automatically. The bot reads the ticket, figures out category and priority, and proposes a routing, but a human confirms it before it actually lands in a team's queue.

If a human still has to review and confirm every single suggestion before a ticket routes, what's actually being saved compared to today? Is confirming a suggestion meaningfully faster than triaging from scratch — and how do you know?

Honestly, not much if it stopped there — but that's not the plan. The confirm step is a one-click glance for anything routine, way faster than reading the ticket cold and figuring out category and team yourself. For the genuinely easy, low-stakes tickets — password resets, "where's my order," stuff like that — it just closes them outright, no human in the loop at all. That's where the actual time savings come from.

Those two answers don't sit together. A moment ago you said: *"It suggests — nothing moves automatically... a human confirms it before it actually lands in a team's queue."* Just now you said: *"For the genuinely easy, low-stakes tickets... it just closes them outright, no human in the loop at all."*

Which one is actually true — does the bot ever act without a human confirming, or not? Which of those two claims gives way?

Both are true and neither cancels the other. Routine, low-stakes stuff — password resets, order status — closes automatically, no human touch. Anything else, the bot proposes a routing and a human confirms before it moves. Those are two different tiers, not a contradiction.

Fair — that's a two-tier design, not a contradiction. Let's stay with that though: what happens when the bot auto-closes one of these "routine" tickets and gets it wrong — say it closes what looks like a simple password reset but was actually a compromised-account report, or an "order status" ticket that was really a fraud complaint? Who catches that, and how?

Not much of anyone, honestly, if we just close it and move on — that's the gap. But the reply itself never goes to the customer without a human signing off first; that's non-negotiable after what happened with compliance last time. Every outgoing message gets individually reviewed by a named person before it's sent, no exceptions, so even on the "auto-close" tier there's a human checkpoint on what actually reaches the customer.

That's another collision with what you said two turns ago. Then: *"For the genuinely easy, low-stakes tickets... it just closes them outright, no human in the loop at all."* Just now: *"...even on the 'auto-close' tier there's a human checkpoint"* — every outgoing message reviewed by a named person before it's sent, no exceptions.

So is there a human in the loop on the auto-close tier or not? Which of those two stands?

Both stand — they're not the same tier doing two things. The auto-close is the bot resolving the ticket itself, no human touch there. But the reply text that goes out to the customer still gets individually reviewed by a named person before it's sent — that's the compliance checkpoint, and it's non-negotiable after what happened last time. So yes: the bot closes the ticket without a human in the loop on the routing/closing decision, and a human still signs off on every outgoing message. Both.

Okay — so the distinction is "no human on the routing/closing decision" versus "human review is still mandatory on the outgoing message." But if every outgoing message on the auto-close tier also needs a named person's individual sign-off before it sends, what's actually different in human effort between that and the "suggest a routing, human confirms" tier? Isn't a human still touching every ticket either way — just reviewing a drafted reply instead of reviewing a routing suggestion?

Not much, honestly — you're right that it's basically the same human touchpoint either way. The difference is just what's being reviewed: a routing suggestion versus a drafted reply. But I'm not willing to cut the reply sign-off to close that gap — that checkpoint is non-negotiable regardless of what it does to the time savings math.

Given that human touchpoint doesn't actually shrink between the two tiers, what number or outcome would tell you this project failed to deliver real value — is there a specific time-saved-per-ticket or throughput figure you'd need to see to justify having built it, versus just... it not moving the needle?
